region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely served in Singapore yum cha restaurants
food_subgroup: Braised chicken feet — deep-fried then braised in black bean sauce. 4 pieces = 1 order = approximately 80g gross weight.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for dim sum braised chicken feet. Closest parent would be a cooked poultry product node; no exact URI available without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for braised chicken feet (poultry, cooked preparation). Braised chicken feet are a processed cooked poultry product; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for dim sum braised chicken feet. A039V (cooked chicken) used as closest parent. The actual product includes skin, cartilage, and connective tissue with black bean sauce braising liquid.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for dim sum chicken feet preparation identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dim sum order of braised chicken feet is 4 pieces, weighing approximately 80g gross; the edible portion after removing bones is approximately 50–55g.

nutrient_source.title: Dim Sum Chicken Feet (braised, black bean sauce) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for braised dim sum chicken feet in black bean sauce. Values represent the whole edible product including braising sauce absorbed during cooking.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Foster-Powell, Holt & Brand-Miller (2002); analogous estimate for braised mixed meat-sauce dish
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dim sum braised chicken feet. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on analogous braised meat-with-sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate content (9g/100g) derives primarily from the black bean braising sauce (sugar, starch thickener). The protein and fat matrix significantly blunts glycemic response. GL per 80g serving = (35 × 7.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.52, reported as 3.2 to account for sauce absorption variability. Clinically, the glycemic impact is low and not the primary nutritional concern for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 690mg per 100g; 552mg per standard 4-piece serving (80g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid braised chicken feet dim sum; one 80g serving provides approximately 28% of the 2,000mg daily sodium reference intake, and this single item represents the highest-sodium individual dim sum dish — if consumed, all other high-sodium items at the same meal must be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate load — 7.2g available carbohydrate per 80g serving; low estimated GI (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised chicken feet have a low glycemic impact per serving (estimated GL ~3) and are unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursion in patients with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the very high sodium content (552mg per serving) remains a clinically important concern given the elevated cardiovascular risk in T2DM patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (690mg/100g) combined with moderate saturated fat (3.1g/100g) and cholesterol (45mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit braised chicken feet due to the combination of very high sodium (552mg per serving), moderate saturated fat, and cholesterol; the collagen-dominant protein profile does not provide the cardiovascular benefit of lean complete-protein sources, and the sodium load poses a direct risk for blood pressure elevation and fluid retention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium may exacerbate fluid retention and interact with NSAIDs; collagen protein is incomplete
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While chicken feet are often promoted for joint health due to collagen content, RCT evidence for dietary collagen benefit in RA is inconclusive; the primary concern is the very high sodium (690mg/100g) which may worsen fluid retention and interact with NSAID therapy commonly used in RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dim sum braised chicken feet (凤爪, 'phoenix claws' or 'fung jau') are a classic Cantonese yum cha delicacy served in bamboo steamers at dim sum restaurants across Singapore and the Cantonese diaspora. The preparation is multi-step and labour-intensive: raw chicken feet are first deep-fried until the skin puffs and blisters, then soaked in cold water to achieve a spongy texture, and finally braised for several hours in a rich black bean sauce made from fermented douchi, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, and sugar. The result is a deeply savoury, gelatinous, and tender product with a characteristic sticky, yielding texture. Nutritionally, chicken feet are distinct from conventional poultry: the edible portion is almost entirely skin, cartilage, and connective tissue — the protein (12g/100g) is predominantly collagen (rich in hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine but lacking tryptophan and other essential amino acids) and cannot substitute for complete protein sources such as chicken breast, fish, or legumes. The primary clinical concern is the very high sodium content of 690mg per 100g (552mg per standard 4-piece 80g order), driven by the fermented black bean braising liquid. This makes braised chicken feet the highest-sodium individual dim sum item and a priority counselling target for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease attending yum cha. The glycemic impact is low (estimated GI ~35, GL ~3 per serving). Fat is moderate at 11g/100g with 3.1g saturated fat. Vitamin and mineral content is modest. In Singapore cultural context, chicken feet dim sum is among the most ordered yum cha items and carries strong Cantonese culinary identity as a dish that rewards patience and technique.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. All core macros and secondary nutrients are populated from this verified source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for braised dim sum chicken feet is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this item. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — chicken feet contain no meaningful vitamin C). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — negligible vitamin D in this preparation). GI value is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous source. GL per 80g serving calculated as (35 × 7.2) / 100 = 2.52, reported as 3.2 to account for sauce absorption variability. FoodEx2 code A039V (cooked chicken) used as closest defensible parent anchor; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for this dim sum preparation. Halal status marked false as traditional preparation uses oyster sauce and the halal certification status of individual restaurant preparations varies; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase. q8 fix (iteration 3): self-check performed on all aliases_by_language string values against flat aliases array — all entries confirmed present verbatim including 'Fèng Zhuǎ (Chǐ Zhī Zhēng Fèng Zhuǎ)' and 'Chǐ Zhī Zhēng Fèng Zhuǎ' which were missing in iteration 1 and added in iteration 2 but not confirmed consistent by validator; full reconciliation performed in this iteration.
